key_insight: Topology-optimized dual-wavelength Si-CdO epsilon-near-zero nanocavities can boost single-photon cross-phase modulation enough to enable mid-infrared quantum nondemolition detection at room temperature.
engineering_relevance: The design offers a solid-state path to compact mid-IR single-photon detectors, sensors, and all-optical gates beyond conventional noise and sensitivity limits.
method: Green's tensor quantization for open, lossy resonant nanostructures
Finite-element quasi-normal-mode simulations of a hybrid Si-CdO nanocavity
Inverse design via free-form topology optimization for dual-wavelength trapping
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a simulated single-photon XPM frequency shift of about 18.4 GHz, corresponding to a fractional pulling of 9.23×10^-5, under a 1.5 μm probe and 3 μm pump configuration.
watch_item: Practical performance may be constrained by fabrication tolerance, material loss in CdO, and the ability to suppress self-phase modulation, thermorefractive noise, and detector jitter in an experimental device.
paper_title: Mid-Infrared Single-Photon Detection via Enhanced Cross-Phase Modulation in Topology-Optimized Epsilon-Near-Zero Dual-Wavelength Nanocavities
